sentences.text: Yeah.
The Hlinka Guard.
Well, these Slovaks-- the Hlinka guards-- took me to a military camp.
And there were a lot of horses in this military camp.
We have to--
No.
I don't remember.
Just that the city's name was Kosice.

And we were supposed to clean all the manure of this-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] stalls where the horses are, and to bring them feeding them, and to wash them and to make-- we were working all day only with these horses.
So we got so disgusted from this smell over there.
And all we got to eat was like bread so hard you couldn't break, you couldn't chew it even.
So like a rock.
And then they bring us to drink some black coffee.
And if somebody was not doing their job, he would get-- he was taken away.
And came back and says, they hit them and they beat them up.
So we did very-- we were there for almost-- more than a week, working there.
It was very hard, and I got very dirty-- my clothes.
And I was unshaved.
I looked terrible.
Then they took us back to the train, put us on a cattle train, and heading home.
When we reached home, by train, Bustino.
I came off the train, I went walking home.
Nobody on the street would recognize me.
When I entered the house-- our house in the store-- my father almost got a heart attack.
It was some kind of a beggar just coming in the store to ask for a handout.
He wanted to chase me out.
And when I said, Dad, it's me, Isaac-- they called me in Yiddish, Isaac, not Emil-- my father almost got a heart attack.
I'm not exaggerating.
He started to cry, and to sigh, oy vey!
Oy vey!
What happened?
Ma, come and see Isaac.
And my ma rushed in, and she started to cry.
And everybody was crying-- my brothers.
So they cleaned me, and they washed me, and they dressed me up again.
And then I started telling the story.
My father wouldn't believe it, what happened.
And he told me, see, because you left our home.
You went away.
You left home.
That's why God punished you, he said to me.
I said, it's not that God punished me.
That's what Hitler wants to do with the Jews.
You look at me, I told him.
That's what's going to happen.
They came into Brin.
I told him what happened in Brin.
I told him what I saw Hitler.
I told him what I saw how the synagogues were burning, how Jewish stores are being destroyed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he says, it's not going to happen here, he says.
That's over there, he says.
So I was again at home.
Business was very-- the Hungarians were already occupying our area.
No more Czechs, no more Russians.
The Czech army invaded this territory.
And they said, it's Hungary now.
From now on, it's Hungary.
It's exactly a few days before I got home, the Hungarian army invaded this territory.
And my father told me, we also had trouble here, because the Hungarians went and invaded Bustino, and we are located just on the border of Hungaria.
There was this river.
And the Hungarian troops had to cross this river.
On the other side of the river, Lithuanian nationalists, called the Sich guard, were hiding, tried to ambush the Hungarians when they entered the city.
And shooting developed.
The whole night they were shooting.
And bullets were flying.
And we had to sleep in the basement, my father is telling me, the whole night.
In the morning we found windows broken from the shooting.
So we didn't have it so good.
But thank God, he says, now the Hungarians are here, it's quiet.
And everything is under control.
But they are doing us trouble with the store.
They come every day and checking, and telling this and all kind of rules, and asking for papers, he says-- [INAUDIBLE] papers.
He says, they want us to have Hungarian citizenship.
And my father told them that we are citizens, because we lived here, and we was born here.
Yeah, but they said, you were almost 20 years out of this country.
You lived in America.
And you are American citizen.
So you are also a foreigner, they said.
You're supposed to apply for Hungarian citizenship.
So they decided to send me to Budapest.
And there I should go to the Jewish community in Budapest.
Because my father-- the store was almost nonexistent.
Very little business.
So we didn't have the money to pay off these big people from the police.
Like, other Jews, they paid them off.
Even they didn't have the papers, if you would have enough money to pay them off, they wouldn't put you on the list that you were a foreigner.
My father was put on the list as a foreigner, because we couldn't pay them off.
So he sent me to this Jewish community in Budapest to ask for some help.

As I came there, I first of all asked they should help me to get settled, to get some rooms, with somebody else-- a roommate, or something-- and get me some job, or to learn some trade, so I can make a living.
And then I told them that my father is complaining that they are being hassled by the authorities over there-- the Hungarian authorities-- that he should apply for citizenship.
And my father cannot afford to pay them off, so I would like to you to help me.
I went to Professor Nemes in the Jewish community, and Professor [?
Tanar ?].
They were the leaders of the Budapest communities.
And they wrote down all the information I gave them, promising to take care of this.
But they said, they cannot do it fast.
It will take time.
And they will try to do something about it.
So I kept asking, for almost every week, and nothing came up.
And this was-- Did you work during this time?
This time, yeah.
I start-- they put me to work on a few places, like in a bakery, which I had to run away because all the rats.
I was sleeping on the sacks of flour where the rats came at night.
And I couldn't stay there, couldn't sleep at night.
I couldn't work.
And a few jobs like this, dead jobs, which I couldn't work.
So, finally, they got me a job as a apprentice in a sign-painting job, which was close to my hobby.
And I liked it.
And I was helping out to install the signs on a store, and how to prepare for the guy to sit down and paint the lettering, you know, on the signs.
So there I used to work for a while.
And this was about in '39, when I got there, in Budapest was winter in '39.
So the whole 1940 went by, and I was asking these people about what happens to these papers.
They said they applied.
They send in for the Hungarian authority-- this was the Minister of Internal Affairs, you know?
They have to apply.
And he said that they don't know.
Maybe already your father got something.
And I have to write home-- because we didn't have a telephone-- to ask him.
I told my father, I spoke to this and that, and they are telling me that they have intervened for you, and maybe you got some papers from them.
And they said, yes, we got something, and they asked me more information.
I had to give them information.
But no citizenship yet.
Just all kinds of information they're asking for the information.
So I used to get from home for Passover, and for our high holiday always a package of goodies-- some cookies, and some baked chickens, and challah, and all kind of goodies, and a letter.
And there was this-- 1941-- 1941 was already time for me to get something.
It was already the month of August went by, and September already.
Almost the days of the holidays are here, and I didn't get anything.
So I was wondering something.
I had a hunch that something was happening there.
But I couldn't find out.
Nobody knew, and I couldn't call up anybody to ask.
I wrote letters.
I didn't get an answer.
So all of a sudden I got this postcard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Out of the blue I got a postcard.
I looked at it.
I couldn't understand, make no heads and tails of it.
Because it's written with a pencil, like, you know, you could hardly able to read the handwriting of my mother.
But she remembered my address, and she wrote down my address where I lived.
I lived in Budapest, it the street was named Delibab Street-- I don't remember. 16, I guess. 16 Delibab Street-- the sixth kerulet.
Kerulet means the district of Budapest.
The address was correct, but the addressee-- I mean, the addresser who mailed this didn't give his name, nor his address.
All I found was a stamp on this from the post office by the name of Chop.
Chop is a city which is located [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Hungary.
What is my mother doing in Chop?
I didn't understand.
So I went--
No, and this postcard-- my mother's telling me in Yiddish, Isaac, we are in a very bad spot.
And say Kaddish up to my father.
Means that he is dead.
And watch not to get to this place.
You hide.
You go in hiding.
Because they are going to get you.
So with this card, I run to this community president, over there-- Pastor and Nemes-- and I started to make a big fuss, started to scream and yell.
What is going?
Look what I got!
They couldn't read it because it's written in Yiddish, and they know only Hungarian.
And I told them what the card says.
But they tried to calm me down.
Don't get hysterical, he says.
They are just in Chop.
Probably they put them-- till they will get Hungarian citizenship, they will keep them in Chop.
Chop is a city, Hungarian city.
They will keep them there.
And they will stay there.
And don't worry.
They will get the citizenship.
They will be OK.
Right?
But I couldn't get over it.
And I was so shocked, and so devastated with this postcard, that I couldn't even go home.
And I couldn't eat, I couldn't sleep.
I was just out of my mind that something has happened here, and I don't know what.
So I was afraid to go home because they might pick me up.
So I was hanging out on the streets.
I was going to see all kind of places where I can stay warm, because it was winter.
And daytime, I would also walk on the streets and passing just time, and just thinking what to do.
I had some little money on me.
But I didn't have enough money to buy food.
So one night, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sitting on a bench in the park, in the city park.
And there, it went dark at night.
And I probably fell asleep on the bench.
And a policeman on a bike went by, saw a guy there, a homeless guy, lying on this bench.
And he picked me up.
He said, lets go.
And he took me to this jail.
They called it [?
Tallinn's ?] [?
House ?], in Budapest.
So over there, in this [?
Tallinn's ?] [?
House, ?] they put me in a large hall.
There were hundreds, or more than 100 people were there, sitting in little groups and discuss, and talking.
And the conditions were very bad over there.
And I was, all the time, with this postcard showing for people why you can make out what is going on here, tell them my story.
And there happened to be a group of guys who were also picked up on the street without an-- I didn't have IDs on me.
So they were picked up.
And they were refugees from Romania without ID cards.
And they told me the story, what is going on.
That from Romania, also, people are being taken to an area in the Ukraine.
Because it was 1941, broke out the war between Russia and Germany.
But he didn't want to take care.
He didn't.
He just delayed the whole thing by promising to do it in certain stages, to a little by little do it, not all of them at once.
But this story of my best friend, [INAUDIBLE] father, who was also on this transport, he managed to come back from this.
He was not killed.
His wife was killed, and one of his daughters killed.
But his son, [INAUDIBLE], was with me in Budapest at that time.
I was in jail.
These Romanians told me the story what happened, that these 20,000 Jews Hitler demanded from Hungary to expedite, to deport-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he complied.
He complied with this order.
No, I didn't know this.
I didn't know.
I just heard the story from them.
So I was already relaxed little bit that I know that my parents-- what my mother's telling me in this postcard-- must have happened during the deportation from our home.
So I could make two and two together.
But still, I didn't know the whole story.
The whole story, only later, after the war.
I found out exactly from this [INAUDIBLE] father, and from other people who were on that transport, who managed to escape.
Because it was very easy, they said, to escape.
There was a train. in [INAUDIBLE] a train was deporting, let's say, 500 Jewish people.
Right?
And then they went to another station, also deporting.
So there was a big train of thousands of people.
But on the whole train was only two guards standing on the train, watching that nobody should escape.
But many people, when the train stopped, made a stop, younger people who didn't have family or something, they escaped from the train.
And they came back.
Either they came back home, or they went to Budapest.
And this guard over there, even didn't know because he didn't come to check who is missing, who's not missing.
Yeah.
So in this jail, I was for a couple of months, I was there.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was asked if somebody can bail me out.
And I told them that I used to work with a company as a sign painter.
And I give their name and address, phone number.
The name of the company was Bajtek.
Bajtek.
His name was Bajtek.
And the name of the company was Bajtek.
B-A-J-T-E-K-- Bajtek.
Mr.
Bajtek came.
And he bailed me out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He bailed me out.
I don't remember, and I didn't ask.
Maybe.
I just was happy that he bailed me out.
He was a very good man.
But over there on the job was a guy, a Hungarian guy, who was very anti-Semitic.
And he was so happy every day.
He was teasing me that today the Germans took another town, another city, and Russians are losing.
And there will be another couple of days it would be in Moscow.
And all Russia will be kaput.
And the Jews will be caput.
And he was even trying to bet at me for a beer.
You bet that tomorrow they'll be in Kiev?
I say, no way.
OK.
He said, a bottle of beer.
And I was losing.
I kept losing.
Because the Russians were losing.
The Germans were winning the war.
He bailed me out.
I came back to work there.
And I was working.
I was working, but under these circumstances, that the more the Russians were losing, the more anti-Semitic the Hungarians got.
That was in '41.
And there was a time when I couldn't work.
Because I was-- yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Either I couldn't work-- I don't remember what happened.
Or I was just-- gave me just off for a couple of weeks.
There was nothing to do.
They told me, you go ahead and stay home for a while.
So at that time, I used to work-- I used to look for what, something to make money.
And I came to a Jewish big restaurant, where German officers used to go there and have dinner with music, gypsy music.
And they needed somebody to help there, in the restaurant.
So the boss of this restaurant put me in the men's room.
When an officer comes in, I should give him the paper, the tissue or a paper.
And then if needs to polish his boots, I can make some tips.
So I made nice money there.
So once-- Always when the officers came, I would do my job.
And we didn't exchange no nothing.
I just would do my job.
And then I was extending my hand.
He would put me in the hand, some coins.
That's it.
Once I was doing it with an officer.
But he was not a Gestapo.
He was just a regular officer, different.
The Gestapo, they used black uniforms.
And he asked me, what's my name?
And I didn't look at him.
I said, Emil.
He says, oh, Emil.
Emil is a German name.
How come?
I said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] like this, I don't know.
I said, I am Jewish, I said to him.
You're Jewish?
He said, Emil is a name I like.
He even mentioned a movie star, a German movie star had this name, Emil.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he says, I like this name.
He says, what are you doing here?
I says, I'm working here.
OK, start all kind of questions he asked me.
And I was not looking at him.
But when I told him I'm Jewish, I looked in his eyes.
And I ask, what is your name, sir?
My name is Fritz.
He looked at me.
He had blue eyes.
Fritz.
Didn't tell you his last name?
No.
Fritz.
And he give me a big tip, the biggest tip I ever got, this officer give me.
So I didn't know what to make out of it.
I forgot this whole incident, the episode.
And then I was working for a while, all kind of jobs.
I went to a market, flea market.
And I would buy [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] food stamps, bread, and all kind of food stamps, buy it from people who need money very-- and then I would sell it.
And I would make money on this.
And I went to Jewish places, where the Jewish area like Borough Park, here, in Brooklyn, where only Jewish people lived.
[INAUDIBLE], they call it.
And there would come, in the Jewish restaurants, all kind of refugees from Poland and from Slovakia.
People who went to Kamenets-Podolsk and they came back.
And they didn't have papers like I didn't have.
And they needed to get these food stamps.
So I give them food stamps for free, only to tell me the story when I found somebody that he was in Kamenets-Podolsk, to tell me exactly what took place.
Yes.
Yes.
Can I tell the story?
[INAUDIBLE].
